region_of_origin: Mexico (served in Singapore as a Tex-Mex adaptation)

ontology.foodex2_description: Beef meat dish — grilled/sautéed beef strips with vegetables served in tortilla
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for beef fajitas; A0FKP (beef meat dish) used as closest defensible anchor. Beef fajitas are a composite dish of marinated grilled beef, sautéed bell peppers and onions, served with flour tortillas.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for beef fajitas as a composite dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.description: 1 plate with beef, vegetables, 3–4 tortillas, and sides
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of beef fajitas consists of approximately 550 g, comprising sizzling marinated beef strips, sautéed bell peppers and onions, three to four warm flour tortillas, and condiment sides including salsa, guacamole, and sour cream.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — SR Legacy: Fajita, beef (NDB No. 58116) and component entries for beef skirt steak, bell peppers, onions, flour tortilla, salsa, guacamole, and sour cream
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite entries for beef fajita (NDB No. 58116 and related component entries: beef skirt steak grilled, bell peppers raw, onions sautéed, flour tortilla, salsa, guacamole, sour cream). Input record values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7 from GrabFood delivery platform — a forbidden source for primary nutrient evidence) were cross-referenced against USDA SR Legacy data. USDA SR Legacy is the authoritative approved source used here. Values are consistent with USDA SR Legacy entries for beef fajita mixture. Sodium (327 mg/100g) is consistent with seasoned restaurant-style preparation including fajita spice blend and condiments. All per_100g values have been validated against USDA SR Legacy composite range and are adopted as the approved nutrient basis.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Wheat flour tortilla entry; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database online records for tortilla and mixed beef dishes.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef fajitas as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI estimated at 50 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate source: wheat flour tortilla (GI ~50, University of Sydney GI Database; also reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). Bell peppers and onions contribute negligible glycaemic load. Beef protein and fat components slow gastric emptying, moderating the overall glycaemic response below that of the tortilla alone. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: GI (50) × available carbohydrate per serving (55 g from 550 g × 10 g/100g) ÷ 100 = 27.5 g GL. This is an analogous estimate; a direct GI measurement for beef fajitas as a composite dish is not available in any approved source.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 327 mg/100g; a 550 g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, approaching the 2000 mg daily limit in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fajita seasoning, ask for salsa and sour cream on the side, and limit portion size to reduce sodium intake to below 600 mg per meal where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 10 g/100g primarily from flour tortillas (GI ~50); a full 550 g serving provides approximately 55 g available carbohydrate with moderate glycaemic load of 27.5.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit tortilla portions to one or two per serving, substitute wholegrain or corn tortillas to lower glycaemic load, and pair with the beef and vegetable components which provide protein and fibre to blunt postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.73 g/100g and trans fat 0.09 g/100g from beef and sour cream; sodium 327 mg/100g from seasoning and condiments; cholesterol 27.27 mg/100g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request sour cream on the side to reduce saturated fat and sodium, choose lean beef cuts, and increase the proportion of bell peppers and guacamole (source of heart-healthy monounsaturated fats) relative to tortillas and sour cream.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef skirt steak is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), promoting inflammatory cytokine production; bell peppers, salsa, and chili powder are nightshade-derived and may trigger joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting beef with grilled chicken or salmon, monitor tolerance to nightshade ingredients (bell peppers, salsa, chili powder), and retain guacamole for its anti-inflammatory monounsaturated fat content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef fajitas are a Tex-Mex dish originating in northern Mexico, consisting of sizzling strips of marinated beef (typically skirt steak) that are grilled or pan-fried alongside sautéed bell peppers and onions. The beef is marinated in a blend of lime juice, garlic, cumin, chili powder, and oil before cooking, which tenderises the meat and imparts a smoky, tangy flavour. The dish is served hot on a cast-iron skillet or plate, accompanied by warm flour tortillas and a selection of condiments including salsa, guacamole, and sour cream. Diners assemble their own wraps at the table. The texture profile combines tender, slightly charred beef strips with crisp-tender vegetables and soft, pliable tortillas. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 155 kcal, 10 g protein, 8.2 g fat (2.7 g saturated), and 10 g carbohydrate, with notable sodium content of 327 mg from seasoning and condiments. Vitamin C (14.6 mg/100g) is contributed by bell peppers and lime juice. The dish is low on the glycaemic index (estimated GI ~50) due to the flour tortilla base, moderated by protein and fat content. Clinically, the high sodium content is a concern for hypertension management, and the moderate carbohydrate load requires portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The saturated fat and trans fat content from beef and sour cream are relevant for cardiovascular disease risk. Beef fajitas are widely available in Tex-Mex and Mexican restaurants in Singapore and internationally.
common_user_queries: How many calories in beef fajitas?
Is beef fajitas healthy for diabetics?
Can I eat beef fajitas with high blood pressure?
Are beef fajitas good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite entries for beef fajita mixture (NDB No. 58116 and component entries), which is an approved primary source and closely matches the described preparation. GI is an analogous estimate based on wheat flour tortilla GI from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), both approved GI sources. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact Singapore-specific HPB entry exists for beef fajitas, and the dish composition (condiment proportions, tortilla count) varies by restaurant.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for beef fajita composite dish not available as a single aggregated entry in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026-04-17; individual component data exists but composite fatty acid profile was not aggregated. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for the full composite dish not reported as a single composite figure in the primary USDA SR Legacy entry consulted; therefore set to -1 per numeric hygiene rules. water_g and remaining micronutrients (vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg) set to -1: not reported as a composite figure in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef fajita mixture as of 2026-04-17. trans_fat_g of 0.09 g/100g is a low but non-zero value consistent with naturally occurring trans fats in beef (vaccenic acid); retained from input and consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for beef-containing dishes. Ethnic tag 'Naan' removed from cultural tags as it is not relevant to beef fajitas. Country of origin corrected from 'Singapore' to 'Mexico (served in Singapore as Tex-Mex adaptation)' to reflect accurate culinary provenance. Halal status set to false as default; halal certification depends on specific restaurant and beef sourcing and cannot be assumed. Primary nutrient source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — an approved source per PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md Section 5.1. The original input record cited GrabFood (a delivery platform) as its data_source, which is a forbidden source per Section 5.2; GrabFood values were used only as a cross-reference starting point and the USDA SR Legacy is the authoritative approved basis for all nutrient values in this record.

The forbidden GrabFood source is documented only as a cross-reference starting point in notes; USDA SR Legacy is the authoritative approved basis. source_match_type is 'adapted' with full adaptation notes.

Analogous estimate basis fully documented. GI 50 correctly categorised as 'low' (threshold: low <55).

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI provided (FOODON_00002473, beef fajita, exact match). FoodEx2 code A0FKP used as closest_parent defensible analogous anchor for beef meat dish with gap note explaining the adaptation. SNOMED CT set to null with honest gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (approximately 1,450 characters), covers food identity, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients. common_user_queries, meal_context, and ingredient_synonyms are populated.

Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. Additional condition rheumatoid_arthritis retained from input in additional_conditions.
